text: The surgeon positions the optic device on the rectus abdominis muscle line, initially placing it one finger below the last strip, but adjusting to two fingers due to gas expansion.

The surgeon uses a zero-degree lens, positioning the optic slightly higher for better visualization. The setup includes a triangle configuration with space for the optic and suction instruments, allowing for efficient maneuvering with one hand.
The surgeon uses bipolar forceps and scissors for dissection, placing a hemoloc clip with the left hand to ensure precise movement while dissecting with monopolar scissors.
The surgeon frees the spleen, tail of the pancreas, and colon on the left side, separating them as a single block from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon visually identifies the tail of the pancreas and the adrenal glands, clearly exposed in the surgical field.
The surgeon continues to dissect the colon, revealing that the kidney structure is largely absent, resembling a lipomectomy rather than a typical nephrectomy.
The surgeon identifies the gonadal vein and makes an incision just external to it, highlighting the decision between incising external or medial to the vein.
The surgeon performs a horizontal incision external to the gonadal vein, followed by a vertical incision under the lower pole of the kidney, taking care to avoid the lumbar vein.
The surgical field shows a vein that is not the gonadal vein, as it is located outside the genital fascia. The low pole of the kidney is not clearly visible in this view.

The surgeon identifies a vein parallel to the gonadal vein located in the mesocolon. They proceed to carefully incise the area, noting the difficulty in locating the low pole of the kidney due to its indistinct appearance.
The surgeon manipulates the tissue to reveal the dilated pelvis of the kidney, identifying the correct anatomical plane near the psoas muscle. The uterus is visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon incises the tissue in the region where the lower third of the kidney is typically located, despite limited visibility of the kidney's volume, and continues to palpate the area for guidance.
The surgeon frees the psoas muscle, demonstrating careful manipulation to avoid pushing back excessively. The surgical field shows the psoas muscle clearly exposed.

The surgeon navigates through thrombotic tissue around the renal pedicle, using a grasping instrument to dissect. The instrument is pushed forward, opened, and then carefully retracted without fully closing to avoid damaging underlying tissue.

The surgeon encounters a small bridge of tissue and coagulates it twice without using ligature. The fascia is cut slightly more, revealing the adrenal glands, which are preserved during the procedure.
The surgeon identifies the adrenal glands, which were not clearly visible on the CT scan. The surgical field reveals the pedicle and the vein adjacent to the adrenal glands.

The surgeon approaches the renal vein, using a grasping instrument to dissect the surrounding tissue. The renal artery is noted to be positioned behind the vein. The surgeon requests assistance to adjust the view or position slightly to the right.
The surgeon identifies a node on the surface, noting the presence of fibrotic tissue, which is typically minimal in this situation.
The surgeon carefully separates adherent tissue at the current level, noting minimal inflammation. The procedure is observed through a two-dimensional vision setup.
The surgeon encounters fibrotic tissue behind the current dissection area and addresses small bleeders using a bipolar grasp. The surgeon dissects lymphatic tissue containing small vessels.
The surgeon identifies the vena, while attempting to locate the renal artery, which is expected to be small due to the non-functional state of the kidney.
The surgeon carefully examines the area around the atrophic kidney, noting that the artery may be very small and requires cautious handling.
The surgeon continues to dissect the fibrotic tissue around the atrophic kidney, preparing to apply a purple hemolog clip to secure a small vessel.
The surgeon identifies a vessel, possibly the main artery, and applies a Hem-o-lok clip. After coagulating the other side, it is confirmed to be a significant vessel.

The surgeon identifies a small artery or branch of the artery and applies a purple clip using a hook instrument. The clip is positioned carefully to avoid catching other vessels, confirming the artery's presence.
The surgeon cleans around the vein, preparing to apply a hemlock clip. The vein is further exposed, revealing a branch that requires careful handling.
The surgeon applies an additional Hem-o-lok clip to a large vessel, ensuring secure closure.
The surgeon identifies a node behind the current structure, noting its appearance. The hilum is positioned downward, and care is taken to preserve the adrenal gland during the procedure.

The surgeon continues to mobilize the kidney, using suction on the left side to deepen the exposure. The right adrenal gland is visible, and the surgeon carefully frees it, noting the presence of vessels passing from the gland to the kidney.
The surgeon requests preparation of a large bag for kidney removal, indicating the kidney will be extracted shortly. The preferred method for removal is through an inguinal incision.

The surgeon observes the long adrenal glands and discusses the incision strategy, noting that muscle fibers can be split without causing pain. The incision is made vertically behind the adrenal glands to preserve them, removing surrounding fat tissue associated with the kidney while avoiding the adrenal glands.

The surgeon performs dissection to separate a small bridge of tissue, encountering vessels that may require passing higher. Despite completing the hilum dissection, attention remains on preserving the adrenal glands, avoiding the edge to minimize bleeding from the adrenal tissue.
The surgeon identifies and prepares to address one more vessel near the kidney, adjusting the port placement to improve access and visibility.
The surgeon carefully coagulates the diaphragm using a monopolar instrument, noting the diaphragm's contraction and movement towards the scissors' tip during electrical stimulation.

The surgeon detaches surrounding fat tissue from the kidney, focusing on the upper pole while leaving the lower pole attached to the uterus. The camera view shifts as the surgeon frees the upper pole structures, preparing to control the uterus.

The surgeon uses a Hem-o-lok clip to secure a structure, noting the absence of the lumbar vein in the visual field. The surgeon attempts to demonstrate the location of the lumbar vein to highlight potential risks, while adjusting the position of the clip for optimal placement.

The surgeon identifies the pedicle, highlighting the anatomical relationship between the spermatic vein and the lumbar vein, which typically originates from the renal vein and encircles the renal artery. The surgeon emphasizes the importance of controlling the lumbar vein if the renal vein is cut lower.
The surgeon encounters a small branch while navigating around the patient's arms, revealing a large vein that requires significant coagulation.

The surgeon incises the lateral attachment of the kidney, preparing for its release. The kidney is visibly freed from surrounding structures, allowing for either a quick or extended removal technique.

The surgeon incises the peritoneal attachment at the upper pole of the kidney, demonstrating the incision made. Traction is applied to facilitate the release of the kidney, with coagulation of vessels as needed.
The surgeon navigates the passage of the ureterostomy, attempting to clean the surgical field as the presence of fat impedes coagulation.
The surgeon performs an incision to remove the kidney, ensuring systematic technique as the procedure concludes.
The surgeon makes an incision in the inguinal area, avoiding the trocar incisions which are positioned too high. The anterior crest is visible near the incision site.
The surgeon makes an incision without cutting muscle fibers, avoiding the need to sacrifice muscle tissue typically required with a port incision.
The surgeon discusses using a bag for kidney removal to prevent leaving any tissue pieces inside. Large scissors are used to make an incision without the need for a trocar.
The surgeon positions the kidney in its anatomical location before opening the retrieval bag, ensuring it is not too medial to facilitate easy placement inside the bag.

The surgeon uses the Parkinson technique, shaking the kidney to ensure it is securely placed inside the retrieval bag. The kidney appears smooth and is positioned downward. The surgeon avoids removing the kidney immediately, instead placing a cover blade inside the bag. Gas remains in the surgical field, and a drain is inserted before the kidney is removed.
The surgeon prepares to remove the kidney from the surgical field, ensuring the drain is properly positioned before extraction.

The surgeon observes the smooth kidney and splits the muscle fibers without incising them, ensuring the incision is sufficiently large for removal. The camera is held to show the external view, and the colon is switched off.
